State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO 95814
Technical Assistance Administration Executive Legal Enforcement Conflict of Interest
916 322 5660 322 5901 322 6441 322 6444

November 8, 1977

Marvin G. Haun, Esq.
Quaresma, Avera, Benya,
Hall & Haun
37232 Fremont Boulevard
Fremont, California 94536

A 77-407

Re: Washington Hospital District

Dear Mr. Haun:

Thank you for your letter of September 16, 1977, in which
you seek advice with respect to the applicability of footnote 4
of the Opinion rendered by the Fair Political Practices Commission, #76-054, City of Pico Rivera (hereafter Pico Rivera) to
the Washington Hospital Authority. It is assumed that the
Authority satisfies the criterion set forth in that opinion
conditioning the status "local government agency" within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act.

The issue presented by your letter is whether the Washington
Hospital Authority may be excused from having to develop a Conflict of Interest Code. We note that the question is ripe, as
the Authority has presently been noticed for a Code by the code
reviewing body. Further, the Authority makes this request with
the approval of its code reviewing body, which has excused the
Authority from proceeding with the development of a Code pending
a reply from the Fair Political Practices Commission.

Upon reviewing the record of the proceedings surrounding
the adoption of footnote #4, the present status of the Authority
creates the situation envisioned by the Commission in footnote
#4.

"4/ At the time this opinion was requested,
the Corporation had not yet issued any bonds.
Since that time, however, all the bonds have been
issued and sold. This fact does not alter our
conclusion that the members of the Corporation

Marvin G. Haun, Esq.
November 8, 1977
Page Two

are public officials, but it may have a bearing
on whether the Corporation must now adopt a con-
flict of interest code and, if so, what disclosure
the code might require. If, for example, the only
functions which the Corporation still performs are
purely ministerial, a code may no longer be re-
quired. See Section 87302 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18751. Even if a code is required, the
disclosure responsibilities imposed on the Corpor-
ation's directors would be limited and specific
in light of the limited role the Corporation now
plays in operating the water system project. See
Section 87302."

The Authority is a bond issuing entity which, at the present
time, has issued and sold all of its bonds. The Authority has
no staff and no operating budget. Its only functions at this
time are to meet once a year to review the Washington Township
Hospital District's approved budget and to review the audit
report of the Authority prepared by an independent certified
public accountant - functions which appear purely ministerial.
Although it is conceivable that the Authority might make deci-
sions which could materially affect a member's financial interest,
based on the information provided to us, it is not foreseeable
that the Authority will be engaged in such decision-making now
or in the future. As you know, the Commission has provided by
Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18751 that certain types of
agencies may receive exemptions from the code adoption require-
ment. Paragraph (b)(1) of this Regulation states:

"(1) That if the agency requesting the exemption
were to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, there
would be no "designated employees.: within the
meaning of Government Code Sections 82019 and
87302(a), subject to its provisions..."

Accordingly, if the Authority were subject to the provisions
of Regulation 18751, it apparently would qualify for an exemption
pursuant to paragraph (b)(1) since, if it were to adopt a code,
there would be no designated employees. However, Regulation 18751
is not applicable in the instant case because the Commission is
not the code reviewing body. Your code reviewing body may wish
to adopt a similar procedure and therefore I have enclosed a copy
of Regulation 18751 for your perusal.

_______________________________

1/ The Washington Township Hospital District's Board is res-
ponsible for the operation and maintenance of the hospital
site and facilities and for the payment of the revenue bonds.

Marvin G. Haun, Esq.
November 8, 1977
Page Three


If you have further questions on this or any other matter,
please do not hesitate to call this office.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Alfredo Flores
                                        Research Specialist I
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

AF:ma
Enclosure

E. A QUARESMA
FRED E. AVERA
ROBERT D. BENYA
ANTHONY J. ELLIS
MARVIN G. HAUN
ROBERT D. ELLIS
MICHAEL J. COHNRICH
DAVID M. O'HARA

LAW OFFICES

QUARESMA, AVERA BENYA, HALL & HAUN
FREMONT, CALIFORNIA 94536

3752? FREMONT BOULEVARD
AT PERALTA BOULEVARD
748-6400

September 16, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
           Chief Conflicts of Interest Division

     Re: Washington Hospital Authority - Request for
         Written Advice

Gentlemen:

     On February 25, 1977 this office, on behalf of the Washington
Hospital Authority, requested written advice as to its status as a
local government agency and its Board of Directors as public officials
a copy of which is enclosed for your reference.

     In response I received a letter from Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock,
Jr. dated March 4, 1977, advising that our request was similar to
one presented on behalf of the City of Pico Rivera, No. 76-054,
pending before the Commission, a copy of which I am also enclosing
for your reference.

     We have recently received from your office a copy of the
Opinion rendered by the Commission in No. 76-054, City of Pico
Rivera, holding that the members of the Board of Directors of the
Pico Rivera Water Development Corp. are "public officials" within
the meaning of the Political Reform Act.

     Assuming the Commission would rule the same as applied to the
Washington Hospital Authority Board of Directors, kindly direct your
attention to footnote 4 of page 6 of said Commission's Opinion where
in it is stated in pertinent part ... "if, for example, the only
functions which the corporation still performs are purely ministerial,
Code may no longer be required. See section 87302 and 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18751 ...".

     Said footnote refers to the fact that at the time the
Commission's Opinion was issued the Pico Rivera Water Development
Corporation's bonds had been issued and sold.

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 16, 1977
Page 2
Re: Washington Hospital Authority - Request for
Written Advice


In the case of Washington Hospital Authority its bonds
were issued and sold on February 1, 1971 and if you will kindly
refer to our Request for Written Advice dated February 25, 1977,
page 2, enclosed, you will note that construction of the project
for which the Authority was created was completed on March 6, 1974
and that since 1974 the Board of Directors of the Washington
Hospital Authority meets annually to review the Washington Town-
ship Hospital District's approved budget and reviews the audit
report of the Authority by an independent Certified Public Account-
ant. On the other hand the Washington Township Hospital District
Board of Directors carries the full burden of operating and main-
taining the hospital site and facilities and the payment of the
Revenue Bonds.

It appears to us that since 1974 the Board of Directors
of the Washington Hospital Authority has and will continue to
function in a purely ministerial capacity.

It is therefore, respectfully requested that we receive
your written advice as to whether or not a Conflict of Interest
Code is required to be adopted at this time by the Board of Dir-
ectors of the Washington Hospital Authority.

With our letter of February 25, 1977 we enclosed copies
of the joint exercise of powers agreement, site lease, project
lease and resolution of issuance of the bonds. If you have any
questions or need any additional information, please contact the
undersigned.

Thank you for your many courtesies and cooperation in this
matter.

Very truly yours,

Marvin G. Haun
MARVIN G. HAUN

MGH:ve
encls.

cc: Mr. Richard M. Warren
Washington Hospital
encls.

F. A GUARRERA
ROBERT E. ELLIS
ROBERT D. BENYA
H. ROBERT HALL
MARVIN G. HAUN
ROBERT D. ELLIS
MICHAEL J.CONNICH
DAVID M.O'HARA

LAW OFFICES
QUARESMA, AVRA, BENYA, HALL & HAUN
FREMONT, CALIFORNIA 94536

27123 EDISON BOULEVARD
AT FOOTHILL BOULEVARD
P.O. 5150

February 25, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Written Advice

Gentlemen:

This office represents the Washington Township Hospital
District of Alameda County, California. We have been asked to seek
your advice as to whether or not the Washington Hospital Authority
of Alameda County, California is a "local government agency" within
the meaning of Government Code Section 82041 and whether or not its
separate board of Directors are "public officials" within the meaning
of Government Code Sections 82048 and 87100 due to the limited capacity
in which they presently function.

On April 1, 1970 the Washington Township Hospital District
and the County of Alameda entered into a joint exercise of powers agreement creating a separate entity, the Washington Hospital Authority.
(See Exhibit "A") The Board of Directors of said Authority is composed
of 5 members, four appointed by the District and one appointed by the
County. No member can be a member of the Board of Directors of the
District or a member of the Board of Supervisors of the County. The
appointments are for staggered terms of four years.

The Authority was created to provide a funding vehicle
for constructing additions to and expansion of the existing Washington
Hospital, owned and operated by the District. The expansion of the
hospital was approved by the electors of the District by a 72% majority.

The Authority leased the hospital sites and facilities
from the District (see Exhibit B Site Lease), issued Revenue Bonds in
the amount of $9,700,000, conducted the improvements thereon and
leased the hospital site, facilities and improvements back to the
District for an annual rental sufficient to pay principal and interest
on the Bonds and all administrative costs. (See Exhibit C Project Lea:

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 25, 1977
Page 2
Re: Request for Written Advice


During the construction period the Authority was required
to employ the District as its Agent for construction of the improvements in accordance with the plans and specifications approved by
the District. The Authority had no staff personnel of its own. All
staff functions were performed by District staff personnel. The
issuance of the Bonds, the approval of the Corporate Trustee, the
Resolution of Issuance, the Site and Project Leases, all contracts
into and all monies disbursed were approved by the District
prior to approval of the Authority.

The proceeds from the sale of the Authority Bonds were
deposited with the Bank of America National Trust and Savings
Association, Corporate Trustee in trust, pursuant to the Resolution
of Issuance (see Exhibit D). All other revenues including rents
of the authority are paid directly to the Corporate Trustee. The
Corporate Trustee disbursed all Authority funds for the construction
of the improvements and now disburses all funds of the Authority
for the payment of principal and interest directly to the Bondholder.
At no time has the Authority or will it in the future directly receive or disburse funds on its own.

On March 6, 1974 the construction of said hospital improvements were accepted as completed by the District as Agent for the
Authority. Pursuant to the Project Lease, the District commenced
payments of rent to the Corporate Trustee on behalf of the Authority.
In accordance with the terms of the construction contracts all cost
and expenses of said improvements were paid. During the course of
construction the District had physical possession of the entire premises and operated and maintained the hospital. After construction
and at the present time the District has full use and benefit of the
entire premises and facilities and pursuant to the Project Lease is
obligated to maintain and operate the hospital, site and facilities
including fire and extended coverage, liability, earthquake and
rental income insurance at its own costs.

Also, pursuant to the Project Lease, the District each
year must take necessary action to include all rental payments in
its budget and make necessary appropriations therefor to service the
Bonds.

Now that the project is complete the Authority Board of
Directors meets annually to review the District's approved budget
and reviews the audit report of the Authority by an independent
Certified Public Accountant.

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 25, 1977
Page 3
Re: Request for Written Advice


Until the expiration of the Leases and payment of the
Revenue Bonds in full, which is projected to occur in 1997, the
District carries the full burden of operating and maintaining the
hospital site and facilities and the payment of the Revenue Bonds.

Although the Authority is in the position of sublessor
and must review from time to time the financial status of the Trust
Accounts and the District's budget it would appear the Authority
functions in a very limited capacity and is not making the kind of
governmental decisions contemplated by Chapter 7 of the Political
Reform Act of 1974.

There is no question the District must comply with the
Act and has in fact adopted its own Conflict of Interest Code more
restrictive than the law requires, which Code is presently being
reviewed by Alameda County.

Very truly yours,

Marvin G. Haun

MARVIN G. HAUN


MGH:vc
Encls.

cc: Mr. Richard M. Warren
Washington Hospital

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration Executive/Legal Enforcement/Center of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

March 4, 1977

Mr. Marvin G. Haun
Quaresma, Avera, Benya,
Hall & Haun
Law Offices
37323 Fremont Blvd. at Peralta Blvd.
Fremont, California 94536

Dear Mr. Haun:

You request written advice with respect to the applicability of the Political Reform Act to the Washington Hospital Authority Board and its Board members. More specifically, you ask whether the Hospital Authority is a "local government agency" within the meaning of Government Code §82041 and whether the members of the Authority's Board of Directors are "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code §§82048 and 87100.

The Washington Hospital Authority is a joint powers entity established for the purpose of issuing revenue bonds and constructing improvements in certain facilities on behalf of the Washington Hospital District. As is typical of such arrangements, certain leaseback provisions are included in the joint powers agreement between the Washington Township Hospital District and the County of Alameda.

The issues raised in your request for advice are virtually identical to the issues presented in the matter of Samuel Siegel, City Attorney for the City of Pico Rivera, Opinion Request No. 76-054. The Commission's determination regarding the issues in Siegel will govern staff advice in the Washington Hospital Authority situation. Siegel will come on for a second hearing before the Commission in early April. I am placing you on the mailing list for interested persons regarding the Siegel Opinion, and you will be apprised of the hearing date and receive a copy of the second draft of Siegel.

Sincerely,

[illegible] Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:arth
